issue_body:
First of all: Thank you for this wonderful, well needed tool!

I'm using the [rad1o badge](https://rad1o.badge.events.ccc.de/), given out by the CCC at the Chaos Communication Camp 2016. This SDR is basically an HackRF clone. issue_title: assertion for entire response body
issue_body:
It's currently not possible to reliably check whether a JSON response is an empty object:

```yaml
response_strings:
- "{}"
```

seems to be the closest we can get, but could easily result in false positives for nested objects.

Even negation (which isn't supported by JSONPath anyway, AFAIK) wouldn't help, as there might not be a fixed list of keys to blacklist.

Thus it seems like we need some sort of `response_body` assertion which checks the entire body? Of course that'll inevitably raise the question of leading/trailing whitespace...

issue_title: how to add motion_blur() to this source code? thank you!
magnitude and casting
issue_body:
Thanks for Pint!  It's a great library, very easy to use, well thought out, and cleanly written.  I was up and running with it in a matter of no time.  I have one hangup, however, and that's with accessing the magnitude value.  For the most part, I find that I can just drop Pint into my projects without having to disturb the way they accomplish arithmetic--except when I need a final value.  I am constantly tripping a ```DimensionalityError``` like so:

    In [1]: import pint
    In [2]: units = pint.UnitRegistry()
    In [3]: float(units.parse_expression("3mm") * 2.2)
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    DimensionalityError                       Traceback (most recent call last)
    <ipython-input-4-6779a77df576> in <module>()
    ----> 1 float(units.parse_expression("3mm") * 2.2)

    /Users/ghall/.current/pyenv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pint/quantity.pyc in __float__(self)
        290         if self.dimensionless:
        291             return float(self._convert_magnitude_not_inplace(UnitsContainer()))
    --> 292         raise DimensionalityError(self.units, 'dimensionless')
        293
        294     def __complex__(self):

    DimensionalityError: Cannot convert from 'millimeter' to 'dimensionless'

I believe this is what the API wants me to use:

    In [4]: (units.parse_expression("3mm") * 2.2).magnitude
    Out[4]: 6.6000000000000005 

I've read the docs, and I think I understand why this dimensionality safe-guard exists, but I also find this part of the API to be pythonicly counter-intuitive.  I would assume that casting a ```Quantity``` to a float (or an int) would always return the magnitude, no questions asked.  When is this the wrong thing to do?  Why is it important to be so stringent about casting?

Thanks for your time and your hard work!

issue_title: java: don't crash on Jython types
issue_body:
The java Serpent library crashes with infinite nesting when used from Jython directly, when passing certain types such as tuples, bytearray etc. Jython passes them as internal objects of type ``org.python.core.Something`` and serpent doesn't recognise these yet.

Fix: teach Serpent (the java lib) about these types.

This will fix the issue reported here https://github.com/irmen/Pyro4/issues/149   (which is not a problem in Pyro)

issue_title: [Feature request] queueing + shuffle
issue_body:
Disc: long post, just skip to suggestions if you aren't up for some squawking.

Hi,

First of all, this is the best mopidy spotify interface around. I have two suggestions which "feels" like depending on one another.

**Whining no.1**: One of the biggest features I miss from the official spotify client is to have the rest of the playlist get queued when I double click on a track, so that it'd keep playing without any further interaction. Right now, the d-click behaviour is to place that track at the top of the play queue in spotmop. the problem: it won't continue playing unless I add the rest of the playlist by hand. The only way to have that is to queue the whole list by clicking "Play" in the playlist details view and skip to the desired track, which is impractical in true sense. Moreover, adding track to the top does that it wouldn't get played as you might already be playing a track somewhere deep in the queue and you need to go all the way back.

**Suggestion no.1**: double click should: clear the queue, add rest of the playlist to the queue, restart playing from the top of the queue.

**Whining no.2**: The player lacks some of the fundamental features a music player should have: play modes and time indicators. Relating to the first issue, it's nearly impossible to have a pleasant experience without constant interaction, since you'd need to skip frequently or hand curate the queue. And it'd actually be a strong "selling point" to have an alternative to infamously lame spotify official shuffling algo. The track time and remaining time indicators are a must too, imho. That'd also allude to a revamp of left frame, possibly with more lines(vertical space) allocated to play controls at the bottom. 

**Suggestion no.2**:  play modes like repeating and shuffling should be implemented for a better playing experience.

issue_title: Reuse docstring from Class doc
issue_body:
I tried a bit more and again I'm stuck.
I usually document the `__init__` method in the class docstring rather than in `__init__` itself.

So I tried

```
import docrep
d = docrep.DocstringProcessor()


@d.get_sectionsf("A")
@d.dedent
class A():
    """
    Docstring from A

    Parameters
    ----------
    a : int
        Some value passed to _func.
    """
    def __init__(self, a):
        self._func(a)

    @d.dedent
    def _func(self, a):
        """
        Doc from _func

        Parameters
        ----------
        %(A.parameters)s
        """
        print(d.params)

        return
```

This gives: 
```
SyntaxWarning: 'A.parameters' is not a valid key!
  @d.dedent
```
However, `d.params` has:
```
'A.parameters': 'a : int\n    Some value passed to _func.', 'A.other_parameters': ''
```

What works instead is just to document the `__init__` method.
But old habits and stuff...

Is there a way around this, or am I again just not seeing how it works?
I guess the `f` in `get_sectionsf` is for `function`, but I don't see the difference to a class, which also has `__doc__`?
Best,
  Thorben

issue_title: image scaling in face recognition
issue_body:
Hi -- 

I'm wondering how images are getting preprocessed before going into `face_recognition_model_v1`.  There's a comment in the code that suggests the face chips are upsampled to 150x150, but I could find exactly where that's happening, or what kind of upsampling is being done.  

Roughly, I'm trying to measure how performance of the model degrades with the quality of the image.  I have a dataset where a number of the extracted faces are smaller than 50x50, so trying to build intuition about how the model might behave at those resolutions.

Thanks
Ben

issue_title: python-keyring-lib should have some form of continuous integration tests
issue_body:
python-keyring-lib has a nice suite of tests and supports various platforms and environments.
[Obvious](https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/84/python-3-incompatibility-in-google-backend) [things](https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/56/cannot-run-the-test-suite-under-python-3) are breaking sometimes, and it would be nice if we can get an early feedback on what's going on.

Github has Travis-CI which is really cool and easy to set up, but it's not available for Bitbucket sadly.
However, considering the extended support of various OS and environments, something like Buildbot running Buildslave on different OS would be great (especially for devs who don't have access to all the OS supported).

Anyway, even without looking for something too much complicated, *something* which could run common tests after each commit and report somewhere here would already be interesting.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/86
- Originally reported by: Jonathan Ballet
- Originally created at: 2013-02-07T09:13:23.404

issue_title: Spaces in URL are not being replaced with %20
issue_body:
Asks does not correctly handle query strings (or paths) with spaces.

I was testing internal application that was containing the query string in the Location header:

```...&response_type=code id_token token&...```

asks followed the redirect but the server gave an error response. 

Requests worked fine. When I compared, requests replaces spaces with %20 with

issue_title: error: argument --content: expected one argument
issue_body:
I was getting this error occasionally while creating zone entries for letsencrypt challenges. Managed to track it down to a content string beginning with a hyphen. The argument parsing library lexicon uses seems to misinterpret that as another argument rather than the value of the content argument.

Worked around it by replacing `--content "$challenge"` with `--content="$challenge"`. Not sure how easy it is to modify the usage output of argsparse, but it might be an idea to recommend this style in the acme-challenge readme examples.

                    for x in xrange(length)]).encode('utf-8')


# Create a directory with UTF8 name

dirpath = os.path.join('/tmp/reproducer', dirname)
os.makedirs(dirpath)

# This passes
for (path, dirs, files) in os.walk(dirpath):

issue_title: write_key argument names
issue_body: Is there a reason that the first argument to `write_key` is called `keyname` instead of `name`? The README suggests that you can pass the same arguments to `write_key` as used to create a `FITSHDR` or write with the `header=` option. issue_title: Please consider creating a Debian/Ubuntu package
issue_body:
Hi,

I just read about python-fitsio on the [astropy mailing list](http://mail.scipy.org/pipermail/astropy/2015-April/003715.html), and I wonder if you would like to create Debian/Ubuntu package?

Ideally, this should be done within the [Debian Astronomy Working group ](https://lists.debian.org/debian-astro/). You could do it yourself, or anyone else can volunteer. As far as I investigated the package, the Debian package creation would be quite easy, so this can even be a task for a beginner.

The only change to the source code I would see now is to build it against the cfitsio library [provided by Debian](https://packages.debian.org/source/unstable/cfitsio) (which is version 3370 in the moment). There is already a pull request #27 for this. @esheldon the package require to be linked to some minimal cfitsio version, so we can make sure that all features are supported.

Please contact me directly, or via the [mailing list](mailto:debian-astro@lists.debian.org) if you need further information. We can guide you through the full process, and also finally upload your package to Debian. Once it is in Debian, it will automatically migrate to Distributions like Ubuntu or Mint.

Best regards

Ole

issue_title: Inline Images In Incorrect MIME Block
issue_body:
In the **_prepare_message** method, inline images are being correctly referenced using Content-ID (CID tag in HTML img, etc.).

However, the image itself is then attached to the "top-level" msg object (which would be correct for a regular attachment), but should be attached to the msg_related MIME part in order for clients to properly embed the image inline.  Some clients handle this more gracefully than others (Thurnderbird shows a "broken image" icon, for example).

Also, order of attachments matter ... so the msg_related.attach() of the htmlstr must happen *before* the msg_related.attach() of the content_object.

issue_body:
I noticed an issue running on a system with full table BGP where calling flush_routes on a separate routing table takes a long time and consumes nearly 6G of RAM which is never released.

Here's a quick way to replicate:

```
pip install pyasn
pyasn_util_download.py --latest
pyasn_util_convert.py --single rib.20161128.2200.bz2 theinternet  # Adjust for the file it downloaded
gateway=$(ip r show exact 0.0.0.0/0 | cut -d " " -f 3)
interface=$(ip r show exact 0.0.0.0/0 | cut -d " " -f 5)
while read prefix ; do ip r add $prefix via $gateway dev $interface ; done < <(awk '{print $1}' < theinternet)
python3 -c 'import pyroute2; pyroute2.IPRoute().flush_routes(table=1000)'
```

issue_title: Consider no-tty handling, again
issue_body:
Following issue #47, reset codes are now emitted even when redirection is taking place (i.e., no tty).
Since colorama calls reset_all when the program terminates, there is now an undesired effect when using colorama and redirection -- a reset code will be emitted along with the redirected output. See:

    > python -c "import colorama; colorama.init(); print('a')" | xargs python -c "import sys; print(sys.argv)"
    ['-c', 'a', '\x1b[0m']

In other words, commands that take output from scripts with colorama will now get an extra ANSI reset code, which they probably don't expect.

* The bahavior before fixing #47: On Linux when there is no tty, reset_all codes were stripped but other ANSI codes were not stripped
* The current behavior: On Linux when there is no tty, nothing is stripped and both reset codes and other color codes are written to the redirected output
* New, suggested behavior: On Linux when there is no tty, all ANSI codes should be stripped

Note that on Windows when there is no tty, all ANSI codes are already stripped.
@tartley noted in #47 that colorama should behave like other unix utilities (like 'ls') and output color codes only on tty. I disagreed, but I changed my mind now.

The change in the code is simple, if I'm not mistaken: change the default `strip` value from:

   strip = conversion_supported

to 

    strip = conversion_supported or not is_a_tty(wrapped)

The impact is that the behavior of colorama on Linux with redirection will change.
Does this make sense? Does everyone agree that colorama should strip the color codes and the reset codes when there is no tty? Does the code change look right?
@tartley, your thoughts?

issue_title: Explore possible SSD1306 optimization improvements
issue_body:
Rather than iterating over the buffer that represents the SSD1306's internal memory doing some cranky calculations on the way, iterate over the image data instead, and use some pre-calculated offsets to update the buffer. Doing this on the PCD8544 code-base saw an improvement of 54 FPS to about 102 FPS, see here: https://github.com/rm-hull/luma.lcd/blob/package-rename/luma/lcd/device.py#L73-L80

The code is also somewhat simpler.

Can also be applied to the SH1106 driver as well.

issue_title: data files installed in the wrong location
issue_body:
The fix for issue 22 introduced a new problem. The data files are now installed in the top folder of the environment.

To reproduce do:

    $ virtualenv-2.6 --distribute venv
    New python executable in venv/bin/python2.6
    ...
    $ cd venv/
    $ bin/pip install clint
    Downloading/unpacking clint
      Running setup.py egg_info for package clint
    Installing collected packages: clint
      Running setup.py install for clint
        warning: install_data: setup script did not provide a directory for 'README.rst' -- installing right in '/opt/tmp/venv'
        warning: install_data: setup script did not provide a directory for 'HISTORY.rst' -- installing right in '/opt/tmp/venv'
    Successfully installed clint
    Cleaning up...
    $ ls
    HISTORY.rst	README.rst	bin		include		lib

distutils even warns about this. In my case my own readme/history files are overwritten by this.

I think to solve the issue you had, you just want to use a MANIFEST.in which will tell distutils to include the files in the sdist. There's no need to actually install them on the target system. If you want to install them as well, I'd use setuptools/distribute include_package_data option (http://packages.python.org/distribute/setuptools.html#including-data-files).

issue_title: How to disable thriftpy's auto decoding?
issue_body:
Recently I found that `thriftpy` would decode hbase keys and values automatically.  For example: 

```
conn = happybase.Connection('example.hbase.com')
table =  conn.table('some_table')
table.put('an_image', {'cf1:content': image_file.read()})
table.put('a_text', {'cf1:content': 'hello happybase!'})

print( type(table.row('an_image')[u'cf1:content']) ) # str
print( type(table.row('a_text')[u'cf1:content']) )  # unicode
```

Is there an elegant way to disable this feature?  Currently I'm doing like this: 

```
row = table.row('some_key')
content = row['cf1:content']
if isinstance(content, unicode):
    content = content.encode('utf-8')
```

issue_title: AtomicReadFileRequest returns nothing if file data is larger than max apdu size
issue_body:
In `appservice.py` `remoteDevice.segmentationSupported` is not initialized properly.  The `get_device_info()` function should be smarter than simply returning a generic DeviceInfo instance.  That should at least reflect the information contain in the APCI of the request, namely the apduSA (segmented response accepted), apduMaxSegs (maximum segments accepted) and apduMaxResp (max response accepted).

This issue was found during an investigation of executing an AtomicReadFileRequest but I suspect that it will present itself for other cases of segmented requests and/or responses.

See thread in the [mailing list archvies](https://sourceforge.net/p/bacpypes/mailman/message/34065658/)

issue_title: Support multi-threading in shared_intermediates
issue_body:
Following up on https://github.com/dgasmith/opt_einsum/pull/43#issuecomment-414661988, the `shared_intermediates` cache is currently not locked.

One option would be to assign the thread id of a unique owner of the cache, such that at least thread conflicts could be detected and reported, rather than mere silent performance degradation.

issue_title: Automatically changing __version__
issue_body:
We need to implement custom command which will help us easily manipulate with versions of package.

What this command should do:

- [ ] Automatically increment `version` string and rewrite file `__version__.py`.
- [ ] Manually create updated version of `__version__.py`.

### Why?
 Generally because i don't want to open `vim` every time when i create new tags.

### How?
Pretty easily:

```
➜  mimesis master ✗ python3 setup.py versioner
Previous version: 0.0.8.
Are you sure? (y/n): y
New version: 0.0.9
Updated! Current version is: 0.0.9.
```

We can integrate this with Travis CI and  then there will be no need to manually change the `__version__.py` file.

```
➜ git tag "v0.0.9"
➜ git push origin master --tags
```

Nothing more. Just push our tags and script does the rest.

issue_title: Handle `import foo.bar as baz` properly
issue_body:
Right now If I have imported something like this

`import asyncio as aio`

Importanize would wrongfully group it into local imports. I've somewhat traced this issue.

1. is_stdlib wronfully return False for this module. And the module argument is 'asyncio as aio'
2. The module argument is wrong because `parse_import_statement` incorrectly parses `as` import.

Few other notes

It would've been easier probably just to use existing python parser like jedi etc. But if you still want to use your own, then I should say that names like `stem` and `leafs` is somewhat misleading, because for AST as for any other tree it's usually called `node` and `children`.

That's maybe (I might be wrong) the root of the problem. You probably only have `ImportNode` and `ImportLeaf` but the truth is that `import foo.bar as baz` should parse to something like
```
Node ImportStatement
    Node BinaryOperator "AS"
        Node Module "foo.bar", Node Label "baz"
```

def close_std_streams(process):
    try:
        process._process_stdin.close() # Local
    except AttributeError:
        process._stdin.close() # SSH

    process._io._handlers[0]._file_in.close()
    process._io._handlers[1]._file_in.close()

appears to work, but there should be a better way.

Niklas

issue_title: Additional Item attributes
issue_body:
What's the recommended way to add additional item attributes?

At the moment (1.8.*):
* For an attribute to be present on all items, it requires to create a new item class and declare the missing descriptors. This item class then needs to be passed to Config on construction.
* For an attribute to be present on a particular item just have to pass it to `Item`: `greeting = Item(help='What you say to the user')`

----

* [ ] A list of attributes in `Item` class that don't cause `AttributeError` if requested, but instead return `None` by default. No descriptors.

issue_title: Board.fen() doesn't output correct en passant square
issue_body:
`Board.fen()` outputs a `-` for the en passant square if there is no legal en passant move, but every reference I can find for the FEN spec says the en passant square should be populated any time a pawn advances two squares, whether there is a legal en passant capture on that square or not.

This is important because it means the FEN generated by python-chess is not compatible with other libraries that follow the standard.

Easy fix on line 2225 in chess/__init__.py
```
epd.append(SQUARE_NAMES[self.ep_square] if self.has_legal_en_passant() else "-")
```
should be
```
epd.append(SQUARE_NAMES[self.ep_square] if self.ep_square else "-")
```

issue_title: Should blank values be allowed ?
issue_body:
If key values of the type `post:<keyname>` or `get:<keyname>` is an empty string, it is still counted towards the limit. Imagine a login form where you type your username and press enter in a hurry, you would reached the `blocked` page instead of seeing an error that says `password field shouldn't be empty`. 

Perhaps this should be a an arg in the `ratelimit` decorator to county empty values towards limit or ignore them.

issue_title: Support filters without input
issue_body:
I have a video file (with audio) and I want to prepend a few seconds of still background (i.e. black) image without sound. I'm trying to do this with `ffmpeg-python`, but no success yet.

First thing I stumbled upon is the missing support for the color input file (see my remark [here](https://github.com/kkroening/ffmpeg-python/pull/62#issuecomment-412622886)), however, I can work around that by creating a black PNG image and loading that in, e.g. using `ffmpeg.input("black.png", t=10)`.

However, now I need to concatenate the background video with the original video, but I can't because the background video doesn't have audio, and concat requires a matching number of audio/video streams. So then I found the `aevalsrc` filter in ffmpeg, which generates a (silent) audio stream, but I can't make it work with `ffmpeg-python`, since it is a filter that doesn't require input. E.g. this doesn't work:
```
stream = ffmpeg.input("black.png", t=10)
stream.filter("aevalsrc", exprs="0", duration=10)
```

Is there any way to achieve such a thing?

issue_title: utils: use `None` instead of 'untrackable' when IP is not detected
issue_body:
### Problem

Using `IPAddressType` is not possible when used together with string value `'untrackable'`.

```python
from sqlalchemy_utils import IPAddressType
# ...
class User(db.Model):
    # ...
    last_login_ip = db.Column(IPAddressType, nullable=True)
    current_login_ip = db.Column(IPAddressType, nullable=True)
    # ...
    @validates('last_login_ip', 'current_login_ip')
    def validate_ip(self, key, value):
        """Hack untrackable IP addresses."""
        if value == 'untrackable':
            value = None
        return value
```

### Proposal

Store `None` value instead of `'untrackable'` in [`flask_security.utils:login_user`](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/4049c0620383f42d37950c7a35af5ddd6df0540f/flask_security/utils.py#L68) when the IP address of user can not be detected.

```diff
diff --git a/flask_security/utils.py b/flask_security/utils.py
index ccd5b0b..114421c 100644
--- a/flask_security/utils.py
+++ b/flask_security/utils.py
@@ -65,7 +65,7 @@ def login_user(user, remember=None):
         if 'X-Forwarded-For' in request.headers:
             remote_addr = request.headers.getlist("X-Forwarded-For")[0].rpartition(' ')[-1]
         else:
-            remote_addr = request.remote_addr or 'untrackable'
+            remote_addr = request.remote_addr or None  # make sure it is None and not ''
 
         old_current_login, new_current_login = user.current_login_at, datetime.utcnow()
         old_current_ip, new_current_ip = user.current_login_ip, remote_addr
diff --git a/tests/test_trackable.py b/tests/test_trackable.py
index 7017754..a0c8906 100644
--- a/tests/test_trackable.py
+++ b/tests/test_trackable.py
@@ -23,7 +23,7 @@ def test_trackable_flag(app, client):
         user = app.security.datastore.find_user(email=e)
         assert user.last_login_at is not None
         assert user.current_login_at is not None
-        assert user.last_login_ip == 'untrackable'
+        assert user.last_login_ip is None
         assert user.current_login_ip == '127.0.0.1'
         assert user.login_count == 2
 
@@ -39,6 +39,6 @@ def test_trackable_with_multiple_ips_in_headers(app, client):
         user = app.security.datastore.find_user(email=e)
         assert user.last_login_at is not None
         assert user.current_login_at is not None
-        assert user.last_login_ip == 'untrackable'
+        assert user.last_login_ip is None
         assert user.current_login_ip == '88.88.88.88'
         assert user.login_count == 2
```

issue_title: default lambda is not handled in Argument.__schema__
issue_body:
Lambdas as default value are supported by Argument class:
https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/reqparse.py#L247

But for swagger only literal value are supported:
https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/reqparse.py#L273

As you can see there is no check for callable - this causes problems when json serializer tries to generate `/api/swagger.json`.
This value later used to fill form inputs in Swagger documentation.
Since lambda can return some special object(date) for example - this can cause additional problems for developer when dealing with Swagger.
I can suggest to add dedicated kwarg(for example: `example`) for Argument class to supply it as default value to fill form input in documentation.

issue_title: Documentation issue regarding mismatching passwords
issue_body:
In [docs/forms.rst](https://github.com/macropin/django-registration/blob/c3dd438a2c8a4b3225d9661a45ea5ef9833d3fd4/docs/forms.rst), it states:

> Because it does not apply to any single field of the form, the validation error for mismatched passwords is attached to the form itself, and so must be accessed via the form's `non_field_errors()` method.

However, the error is actually attached to `password2`, [e.g. see tests](https://github.com/macropin/django-registration/blob/c3dd438a2c8a4b3225d9661a45ea5ef9833d3fd4/registration/tests/forms.py#L48-L53)

issue_title: Upgrade guide in documentation & Releases page conflict with each other
issue_body:
The [upgrade guide in the documentation](https://github.com/macropin/django-registration/blob/9ba4cc7f1cfc524b980739e8d96470848fd6986a/docs/upgrade.rst) and the [information on the Releases page](https://github.com/macropin/django-registration/releases) conflict with each other. For example:

  - The documentation claims that 1.2 changes the parameters of the register method in RegistrationView; this is not mentioned on the Releases page
  - The Releases pages claims that 1.2 requires a `base.html` template; the documentation claims that 1.1 requires it.
  - The documentation claims that 1.1 requires HTML email templates. The Releases page does not mention this, but claims that at least one HTML email template can be disabled by setting `REGISTRATION_EMAIL_HTML` to False. The documentation does not mention `REGISTRATION_EMAIL_HTML` at all.

I think it would be easier for people to upgrade (and for everything to be maintained) if the changelog and backwards incompatible changes were stored in one (and only one) location.

# {'id': u'56a3f0dd1d41c81ff5541f09', 'assets': [<Asset: Asset object>]}
json.dumps(data)
```

There is a workaround, but it is not very useful. Also it is not possible to use ```depth``` option for this scenario.

```python
class BundleSerializer(drfm_serializers.DocumentSerializer):
  assets = drf_serializers.ListField(child=drfm_serializers.ReferenceField(Asset))
  class Meta:
    model = Bundle

data = BundleSerializer(b).data
# {'id': u'56a3f0dd1d41c81ff5541f09', 'assets': [u'56a3f0cb1d41c81ff5541f08']}
json.dumps(data)
```

I think about separate implementation of ListField/DictField serializers, but maybe there is another solution?

issue_title: Proactively refresh tokens before they expire
issue_body: In order to prevent errors around tokens becoming invalid, the SDK should pre-emptively refresh access tokens *before* making API calls rather than waiting for a 401 response from the Box API.  This will necessitate some interface changes to the OAuth2 class, and will be a breaking change in the 2.x release.

issue_title: Request: to_bytes and from_bytes variant of ndarray.save and ndarray.load.
issue_body: Mxnet has convenient `ndarray` (and `ParameterDict`) save/load functions, but they always go through files specified by a path. Because of a serialization pipeline my project is using, it would be convenient to have a helper utility for `to_bytes` and `from_bytes` that operates just like the current `ndarray.save` and `ndarray.load` utilities, but rather than saving to a file, `ndarray.to_bytes` would return the byte stream that `save` would write to file and `ndarray.from_bytes` would return the dict/ndarray from a bytestream that would have been loaded by the file with the corresponding bytes.

issue_title: Don't wheel packages installed from VCS urls
issue_body:
If a user specifies a git package URL to install, it will get wheeled with whatever version is in `setup.py` for the package.

However, there are many cases where this is undesired behavior:

* Installing a commit on top of `master` which is ahead of the latest release.
* Testing a feature branch where the version hasn't been changed to something unique.
* Testing a feature branch where you use a unique version, but forget to change the version when adding more commits on your feature branch.

I think most of these cases would be unexpected for the regular developer.

This has the potential to pollute your cache with versions that don't match the actual version. Especially on CI, this can cause problems.

Here's a currently-failing test: https://i.fluffy.cc/Xr3wxKM7cPb5tvMLnGBc9H9F2QV5ptjn.html

issue_title: Engine looks for git also in non dev installations
issue_body:
To build the version number we check if the OpenQuake Engine is installed via git:

```python

def git_suffix(fname):
    """
    :returns: `<short git hash>` if Git repository found
    """
    try:
        gh = subprocess.check_output(
            ['git', 'rev-parse', '--short', 'HEAD'],
            stderr=open(os.devnull, 'w'), cwd=os.path.dirname(fname)).strip()
        gh = "-git" + decode(gh) if gh else ''
        return gh
    except:
        # trapping everything on purpose; git may not be installed or it
        # may not work properly
return ''
```

This is usually fine, with the except of macOS where, even if `git` call is in a `try / except`, macOS complains about the missing git command and propose the user to install Xcode.

We should not depend on Xcode for non development installation. We should check if `.git` folder exists before running the `git` command.

issue_title: celery must be used when use_celery is true
issue_body: Unfortunately, this is not necessarily the case, because the support for celery is implemented in terms of a HORRIBLE HACK, i.e. the decorator `parallel.litetask` is monkey-patched with the `oqtask` decorator at runtime: depending on the order of the imports, the decorator can be patched too late. For instance, an innocuous refactoring like the one in https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/pull/1978, making the engine.logs module dependent *indirectly* on commonlib.parallel, has the net effect that celery is no more used, because the task functions are imported before the patch. The solution of course is to remove the horrible hack. This is now possible.

issue_title: Support offset/limit pagination strategy
issue_body:
Currently used `page[number]`/`page[size]` pagination strategy is less flexible than `offset`/`limit` strategy. Getting page by `number` and `size` is easy and efficient with `offset`/`limit`, but getting data given offset and limit can be quite inefficient if we can only set page.

For example, the worst case. We want to get `x` rows with offset `x - 1`. We need to get `2x - 1` rows and cut requited locally. If we were using `offset`/`limit` pagination strategy we would have downloaded almost twice less data from our server.

It would be better if `flask-rest-jsonapi` supported both strategies.

issue_title: buffer size for multipart s3 downloads
issue_body:
I noticed recently that for a large download, the [awscli](https://github.com/aws/aws-cli) (`aws s3 cp s3://...`) was faster than using `boto3.s3.transfer.MultipartDownloader`.

After running a few tests of downloading an 8GB file, it looks like maybe the size of the I/O buffer [here](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/2d371d60b6b08b39fabbd60f1c2f2d1277c81de0/boto3/s3/transfer.py#L524) may have something to do with it.  I don't understand why, but making that buffer size larger (e.g., 256KB or 1024KB instead of the current 16KB) seems to improve download speeds consistently for me.

Perhaps that buffer size should be increased, or maybe just made configurable?  I don't understand the pros and cons other than that making it larger seems to help for my use case.

Times for downloading an 8GB file from S3 to a g2.2xlarge instance (I just changed the number in the line of code mentioned above):

- 100 seconds with 1024KB buffer
- 106 seconds with 256KB buffer
- 118 seconds with 16KB buffer (current boto3 code)
- 256 seconds with 4KB buffer

Code for testing:
```
import time
import boto3
import logging
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

t0 = time.time()

logging.basicConfig(level='DEBUG')
logging.getLogger('botocore').setLevel('INFO')
client = boto3.client('s3')

config = boto3.s3.transfer.TransferConfig(
    multipart_threshold=64 * 1024 * 1024,
    max_concurrency=10,
    num_download_attempts=10,
    multipart_chunksize=16 * 1024 * 1024,
    max_io_queue=10000
)

config = boto3.s3.transfer.TransferConfig()
transfer = boto3.s3.transfer.MultipartDownloader(client, config, boto3.s3.transfer.OSUtils())
transfer.download_file('bucket-name', 'path/to/big/file/foo.npy', 'foo2.npy', 8000000000, {})
print("TIME: {} SECONDS".format(time.time() - t0))
```

I previously mentioned this [here](https://github.com/boto/boto3/issues/409#issuecomment-224071473).

issue_title: flatten() produces incompatible dotted names for list
issue_body:
flatten() produces dotted names with a ending '.' for a sequence type, p.ex. `answer.0.`
When using these names with set_value() this leads to an exception:
`AssertionError: Can't call 'set_value' on a leaf node.`

sample code:
```python
import colander

class Schema(colander.MappingSchema):
    answers = colander.SchemaNode(
                colander.Sequence(),
                colander.SchemaNode(colander.String(), name='answer'),
                default=[u'1',u'2',u'3',u'4'],
                )

schema = Schema()

d1 = schema.serialize({})
print 'data:', d1

d1_flat = schema.flatten(d1)
print 'flatten:', d1_flat

# pick one dotted name
dotted_name = d1_flat.keys()[0]
print 'a dotted name:', dotted_name

# this works:
schema.set_value(d1, 'answers.0' , 'test')
print d1

# dotted names have a ending '.' which confuses set_value
schema.set_value(d1, dotted_name , 'test')
print d1
```

issue_title: Configparser does not respect order of config files with includes
issue_body:
When you have an include statement in the user `.gitconfig` there content will take precedence over the local `.git/config` of the repository.

E.g. the following code:
```python
        entry = repo.config_reader().get_value(name, entry) 
```

With the following config setup:

`~/.gitconfig`:
```ini
[include]
    path = ~/.gitconfig-local
[gitlab]
    url = url1
```

`~/.gitconfig-local`:
```ini
[gitlab]
    url = url2
```

`~/.git/config`:
```ini
[gitlab]
    url = url3
```

Will return `url2` instead of `url3`.

issue_title: Deleted instances still appear in formfields (Heisenbug?)
cryptography should be a test dependency
issue_body:
The `cryptography` package is never imported by the `nexmo` package; it is only imported by `test_nexmo.py`. Therefore it should be included in `setup.py` in `tests_require`[1] rather than `install_requires`. That way users who just want to use `nexmo` without running its tests won't end up unnecessarily installing `cryptography` when they `pip install nexmo`. This is especially important because installing cryptography often requires a C compiler, which may not be available in some environments, this also takes a lot longer, bloats their environment with unneeded dependencies, etc. (You can also add a `test` entry under `extras_require`[2] so that users who do want to install the test dependencies can run `pip install nexmo [test]`.)

[1] http://python-packaging.readthedocs.io/en/latest/testing.html
[2] https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30239152/specify-extras-require-with-pip-install-e

issue_title: Testing requests with non-deterministic json.dumps

The static maps API takes urlencoded GeoJSON data. We want to use `responses` to test that our URL is being constructed correctly. But python dictionaries have non-deterministic order so their json.dumps string may not match our mocked URL.

Do we:
* enforce sort order with sort_keys=True? Seems heavy handed to do that just for the sake of testing.
* create multiple mocked URLs for all permutations of the dictionary order? Not very appealing.
* somehow get responses to accept wildcards? Not currently possible it appears.

Any other options? 

cc @sgillies

issue_title: Accessing Wyoming Soundings no ICAO Station ID
issue_body:
So I was attempting to access data from March of 1976 from the University of Wyoming Sounding archive and when going back that far, not every station is associated with a three-letter ICAO ID on the site. A number of the stations are listed by number. Okay not terrible, but not great. I put the number in instead of the station ID and it finds the right data file, but due to the lack of a Station ID, the parsing info from below "Station information and sounding indices" it doesn't work correctly (http://weather.uwyo.edu/cgi-bin/sounding?region=naconf&TYPE=TEXT%3ALIST&YEAR=1976&MONTH=03&FROM=0400&TO=0400&STNM=72349) compared to (http://weather.uwyo.edu/cgi-bin/sounding?region=naconf&TYPE=TEXT%3ALIST&YEAR=1976&MONTH=03&FROM=0400&TO=0400&STNM=72456) the different is in the lack of the "Station Identifier:" line in the 72349 station file.

There might be a relatively easy way to make the parsing of that first line more amenable to working with a set of data that doesn't contain a Station Identifier line. The following code will give the error that results from the previous description.

```python
from datetime import datetime

from siphon.simplewebservice.wyoming import WyomingUpperAir

date = datetime(1976, 3, 4, 0)

data = WyomingUpperAir.request_data(date, '72349')
```

issue_title: Timezone not picked up in blog archive plugin
issue_body:
Is there some configuration item I am missing or may have done incorrectly to have the archive queries be timezone unaware?

First, thank you very much for a nice blog in DJ CMS.  Second, I hope this isn't a dumb question, I tried searching through the open / closed issues, but didn't see this specific issue referenced.  My apologies if it's obvious.

With the latest release 0.8.12, Django version 1.8.18, djangocms 3.4.5 I tried to create a few blog entries (currently it is the month of November).  

I copied and made minor style changes to the templates in my django cms project directory.  I noticed though that when I added the archive plugin to a static placeholder set up for the side bar that the month was off by one.  Posts made in November were getting displayed in the template incorrectly as October posts in the archive.

Note that I didn't change any logic in the template.   I added a line to output the list of dicts showing what the content what in the archive template.  This gave the following date.

```
[{'date': datetime.datetime(2017, 11, 1, 3, 11, 42, 187480, tzinfo=<UTC>), 'count': 4}]
```

The template though renders this as:

```
October ( 4 articles )
```

And just outputting the date as {{ month.date }} in the archive template within it's for loop:

```
Oct. 31, 2017, 11:14 p.m
```

I am using the NY timezone (USE_TZ and the timezone are set correctly in settings.py file), so I was curious why it would render all of these articles published in November as October?  I tried to insert the following lines into the render method of `BlogArchivePlugin` in `cms_plugins`:

```
        qs_dates = qs.get_months(queryset=qs_post.published())
        ny_tz = pytz.timezone('America/New_York')
        for date in qs_dates:
            if 'date' in date:
                date['date'] = date['date'].replace(tzinfo=ny_tz)
        context['dates'] = qs_dates
```

This gives the following changes in the dates rendered in the template:

```
[{'date': datetime.datetime(2017, 11, 1, 3, 22, 14, 720838, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' LMT-1 day, 19:04:00 STD>), 'count': 4}]

Nov. 1, 2017, 4:18 a.m.

November ( 4 articles )
```

So that's correct (or at least I think it is, I'm not sure why the hour/minutes are not just round numbers like a minute after midnight?).  Is there some way to configure the timezone for django cms blog to pick it up for displaying archives?

issue_title: OffsetRequest empty response
issue_body:
The comment and ensuing logic in the `SimpleConsumer`'s [`OffsetRequest`](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bb8363392f665b4e322f5e7bb7e540df31bea3cd/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L602-L615) success handler might be flawed. It was never quite clear to me under exactly what circumstances Kafka would return an empty array and error code zero for this request, but I think I've discovered some more information about it. Specifically, when there is more than one log segment on the partition in question, sending a unix timestamp instead of one of the magic values `-1` and `-2` actually causes Kafka to return a non-empty offsets array. When there is one segment on the partition, the array is empty.

I was testing whether I could get Kafka to give me the segment start offset for a given Unix timestamp. My local `log.segment.bytes` was 1GB, and I had only written about 7k small messages to my local topic. At that point, I'm pretty sure there was only one segment per partition. Supplying a unix timestamp in milliseconds to `reset_offsets` resulted in the [fallback logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bb8363392f665b4e322f5e7bb7e540df31bea3cd/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L602-L615) getting hit. I then changed the `log.segment.bytes` to 10000 bytes, restarted my local cluster, and produced tens of thousands of messages to the topic. This gave me reasonable certainty that there were at least two log segments on my local partitions. Retrying the same `reset_offsets` with a Unix timestamp then yielded a non-empty offsets array.

We should determine whether the [fallback logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bb8363392f665b4e322f5e7bb7e540df31bea3cd/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L602-L615) is still appropriate given this new information, or if it makes sense to treat an absence of offsets in the response as equivalent to `-1`/earliest available.

**PyKafka version**: 2.4.0
**Kafka version**: 0.8.2.1

issue_title: Add option to set the method for aperture photometry measurements in the PSF photometry loop
issue_body:
Right now the PSF photometry machinery depends on doing CircularAperture photometry to make initial flux estimates.  That makes sense for many use cases, but there are some situations where you don't want to do that.  E.g., if your model is highly elliptical, or you know the image is very crowded, or you just don't like the default aperture sizes.

So I'd suggest taking the code that currently does it (For example https://github.com/astropy/photutils/blob/97e38c3e507871af5d18219012b345c15509b9ce/photutils/psf/photometry.py#L256 , although it also appears 2 other places), and replacing it with a new attribute `aperture_photometry`, a la the `finder` and `group_maker` attributes.  The current situation could be left as the default (It could just be a function given that it's really quite simple), but then the user has the option to swap in there own way of doing it.

Does this make sense to you @mirca ?  (I can try to implement it myself if you don't think you'll have time, but either way it'd be good to get your opinion...)

issue_title: Does skimage have a function that does the reverse of label2rgb?
issue_body:
I find myself in need of a function that produces a grayscale / one-channel matrix containing integers that represent several classes in an RGB mask for pixel-wise segmentation.  For example, the RGB mask has been labeled with red (object A), green (object B), blue (object C), and black (background) and I'd need those converted to 1, 2, 3 and 0.

**label2rgb** seems to do the reverse of this, which would be convenient for viewing a prediction, but I couldn't find any function in **module:color** that would do the opposite.

issue_title: Error in ssim equation in skimage.measure compare_ssim ?
issue_body:
Hi,
inside the source code for the compare_ssim function (https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/measure/_structural_similarity.py#L189-L204) I think there is an error in the equation for the SSIM.

Currently in _structural_similarity.py the equation is defined (lines 201 onwards): 
```
A1, A2, B1, B2 = ((2 * ux * uy + C1,
                       2 * vxy + C2,
                       ux ** 2 + uy ** 2 + C1,
                       vx + vy + C2))

D = B1 * B2
S = (A1 * A2) / D

# to avoid edge effects will ignore filter radius strip around edges
    pad = (win_size - 1) // 2
# compute (weighted) mean of ssim
    mssim = crop(S, pad).mean()
```

But I think B2 has been defined incorrectly (by comparing to Wang 2004).
currently `B2 =vx + vy + C2`
But comparing back to Wang 2004 shouldn't it be: `B2=vx**2 + vy**2 + C2` ?

This makes a small different in the resulting MSSIM.

issue_title: AppVeyor should respect NUPIC_CORE_COMMITISH
issue_body:
Relates to https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/2965

NuPIC copes with git tagged releases, pip installing a nupic.bindings wheel file from PyPi. But it doesn't cope with iterative builds working through the .nupic_modules NUPIC_CORE_COMMITISH sha labelling.

This issue is to change the AppVeyor (AV) Yaml script to duplicate the final nupic.bindings wheel file generated for use with release _and_ iterative builds. Adding an upload to AWS of a commit sha labelled wheel file for use with iterative nupic AV builds.

issue_title: release v1.0.0a3
Documentation is unhelpful for complete newcomers
issue_body:
I’ve tried to figure out whether transaction could help me in one of my projects, and the documentation left me completely baffled. It seems to be mostly a bunch of maintainer notes?

Things I noticed as a first-time reader:

- The first sentence is “Transaction objects manage resources for an individual activity.” – that doesn’t tell me any more than the package name.
- The first section is a ZOPE  compatibility notice. I guess at this point you lose 90% of readers because it makes the lib look like a ZOPE implementation detail.
- Next some low-level concepts are outlined without any explanation how they fit together.
- And with that you’re thrown into the meat of the documentation that doesn’t make any sense whatsoever without understanding what the lib is about and how things fit together.
- Especially egregious are the chapters on how to write DataManagers and ResourceManagers without explaining what they are (I still have no clue) or why I’d want to implement them.
    - Bonus points for saying `This transaction.tests.examples.DataManager class provides a trivial data-manager implementation and docstrings to illustrate the the protocol and to provide a tool for writing tests.` while there isn’t a single docstring beyond `Used by the 'datamanager' chapter in the Sphinx docs.`. :)
    - it would also be nice to [link](https://github.com/zopefoundation/transaction/blob/master/transaction/tests/examples.py) to it


All that to say is that I care because I know that most of the zope libraries a best of breed and it would be a pity if people re-implemented their functionality (poorly), just because they couldn’t figure out how to use them.

I understand this issue is not just one issue, so feel free to handle however you want it.

issue_title: allow store to receive a collection as python list instead of rdf:first/rest triples
issue_body:
At the moment `rdflib` provides a RDF collection as `rdf:first/rest` triples. A store, which really requires a collection (i.e. list), has to parse such triples again. This is inconvenient and slow.

Would it be possible to add a configuration option to the store interface, which would say that a store accepts collections as lists and then a parser would provide any RDF collection as a Python list?

issue_title: Remove some/all default prefix bindings
issue_body:
In https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/namespace.py, there are currently some prefixes bound by default in `NamespaceManager`.

At least one of them, `xml`, should be removed, since that isn't an RDF vocabulary at all (but rather an old artifact stemming from RDF being nigh synonymous with RDF/XML in the past). This unnecessarily clutters all serialization output.

The other ones, `rdf`, `rdfs` and `xsd` are more relevant of course. It is still the case that neither `rdfs` nor `xsd` are used in every piece of serialized data though (schema.org comes to mind). And even if `rdf:type` is close to ubiquitous, it doesn't necessarily warrant the prefix being bound by default.

My thought is to remove all but `rdf`, unless anyone can argue for something else. They are relevant, AFAIK, only to serializations (and for relying on implicit bindings in SPARQL queries). In these cases, the source data and/or explicit bindings ought to give enough control over this.

I'll heartily do the removal; I just want to run it by you first. Is there any (conceivable) real usage depending on one of more of these being bound by default? Even if there are, it could be argued that such practice would be obscure, and that in any case, RDFLib 5 can mark a departure from that. Such usage might just as well expect `owl` or `skos` to be bound as well (or `dc`, `foaf`, `schema`...).

Thoughts?

issue_title: Fourier-space Sqrt GSObject/SBProfile (proposed contribution)
issue_body:
I'd like to implement a the Kaiser algorithm for coaddition (see [Zackay & Ofek 2015](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2015arXiv151206879Z)) within GalSim.  I think all this requires is a Fourier-space `sqrt` operator on GSObjects, since all of the other operations (autocorrection and sums) already exist.

 - I'm thinking the `AutoConvolve` or `AutoCorrelate` `SBProfiles` look best as as examples to start from, but I'd love any recommendations for a simple Fourier-space only operator.
 - `Sqrt` probably isn't an appropriate name, since it's not a sqrt in real-space.  But it's also not an operation that has a name in real space.  Any other recommendations?

issue_title: ansible-tower-cli doesn't trust system CA certs
issue_body:
It seems that ansible-tower-cli uses request's cacert.pem.

However, if you have a certificate signed by an internal CA, that's unlikely to be in that bundle. The standard practice for the majority of apps is to trust the system CA (curl, wget, etc. all manage this). 

You can override request's CA bundle using REQUEST_CA_BUNDLE, but need a way to allow that to be configurable as part of the ansible-tower-cli app.

Our previous tower cli install works fine (ansible-tower-cli 2.3.1 with requests 2.10.0) but the current tower cli install doesn't (ansible-tower-cli 3.0.0 with requests 2.11.1) - it is possible that someone did something manual to get 2.3.1 to work, however.

issue_title: Optimizing the control flow from `Experiment` to `Results`
issue_body:
In the current implementation, there are three intermediate layers of steps that are nearly always executed upon invocation of a particular analysis method of `Experiment` class:

 - first, a method of `Experiment` class (such as `Experiment.delta()` for example) is called,
 - then an analysis specific `_function_` (a local one to `experiment.py`, such as `_delta_all_variants()`) is called from that method, and lastly,
 - this function calls a `*_to_dataframe_*` function, which is again analysis-specific.

Thus, the details of analysis flow are spread over three independent levels of abstraction, making each of them not quite flexible and partially causing functionality and code duplication.

issue_title: Resolver eating exceptions

issue_title: Remove dependency link on django-autoslug
issue_body:
As it is, `pip install --process-dependecy-links resolwe` fails when it is run the second time since pip uses the cached wheel it built the first time Resolwe was installed. Hence, it doesn't install Resolwe from the sdist release, which supports dependency links, but from binary/wheel release which doesn't support dependency links.

To resolve this, remove dependency link on django-autoslug and release a forked version on PyPI if the [issue](https://github.com/neithere/django-autoslug/pull/18) is not resolved by the next release.

issue_title: DirExists and Not matchers produce poor feedback
issue_body:
I have a test that includes the following line:

```
self.expectThat(working_dir, Not(DirExists()))
```

When this assertion fails, the feedback I get isn't very readable:

```
'/tmp/tmpBNe_N9' matches MatchesAll(MatchesPredicate(<function exists at 0x7f0835277578>, '%s does not exist.'), MatchesPredicate(<function isdir at 0x7f0835277668>, '%s is not a directory.'))
```

This can be reproduced with the following:

```
>>> import os
>>> os.mkdir('/tmp/hello-testtools')
>>> from testtools.matchers import Not, DirExists
>>> matcher = Not(DirExists())
>>> mismatch = matcher.match('/tmp/hello-testtools')
>>> print(mismatch.describe())
'/tmp/hello-testtools' matches MatchesAll(MatchesPredicate(<function exists at 0x7f0835277578>, '%s does not exist.'), MatchesPredicate(<function isdir at 0x7f0835277668>, '%s is not a directory.'))
```

This is because DirExists is simply a function that returns an instance of MatchesAll:

```
def DirExists():
    """Matches if the path exists and is a directory."""
    return MatchesAll(
        PathExists(),
        MatchesPredicate(os.path.isdir, "%s is not a directory."),
        first_only=True)
```

and does not customize the `__str__` method, which means we get the default MatchesAll __str__ representation.

Being able to create new matcher types with a simple function is 'A Good Thing' (tm), but I wonder if we need a way to customise the `__str__` representations of these new matchers.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Subscribe experimental doesn't appear to be retrying on retryable exceptions, also doesn't surface the error.
issue_labels: [{'id': 139685153, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20pubsub', 'name': 'api: pubsub', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 486182624, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/release%20blocking', 'name': 'release blocking', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'd93f0b', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Full context at https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/4841#issuecomment-386988007 downwards.

gist with logs: https://gist.github.com/psalaberria002/e91956d94e82d706d65da7111a40ca24

Possible culprits:

1. `send()` and `recv()` may actually error on `next(self.call)`, but if that was a *retryable* error that somehow re-bubbled, it would not trigger any callbacks.
2. `bidi` uses `rpc._wrapped` because `api_core` doesn't appropriately expose `add_done_callback`. This means that bidi unwraps all of the retry logic.

This doesn't explain *why* retrying a perfectly normal looking error somehow still leads us to a broken stream, unless the newly opened stream also has an error. But it's unclear why that wouldn't happen when calling the initial RPC.

issue_title: Storage: Downloads give incorrect not found error if object overwritten between calls
issue_body:
Downloads from GCS currently seem to be accomplished as a two step process:

1. Fetch the current object's metadata, including a generation number.
2. Using the generation number returned in step 1, attempt to download the object.

Sample download showing an example of this:

```
client = storage.Client()
bucket = client.get_bucket('yarbrough-test')
blob = bucket.get_blob('test.txt')
blob.download_to_filename('/tmp/test.txt')
```

What this does:

GET /storage/v1/b/yarbrough-test?projection=noAcl
GET /storage/v1/b/yarbrough-test/o/test.txt
GET /download/storage/v1/b/yarbrough-test/o/test.txt?generation=1511999426608623&alt=media


The problem:

In the case where the object has been overwritten by a new generation between steps 1 and 2, a "not found" error would be reported to the user even though at no point did an object with the specified name not exist.

Steps to reproduce:

1. Create a GCS bucket. Do NOT enable versioning.
2. Start a script which rapidly overwrites an object with a new generation.
3. Start a second script which repeatedly downloads the object.

You are likely to eventually run into a "404", although an object exists at all times.

How to fix:

I suggest this be fixed by retrying the download step if it returns with a 404 after the metadata is successfully fetched, unless the user explicitly asked for a particular generation number. Alternately, I might suggest skipping the metadata fetch step entirely, unless the user asks for some piece of metadata, which would significantly reduce the latency for small downloads.

issue_body: The code samples at https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/python-client-migration show similar code before and after the big API changes in 0.28.  However, the before and after samples are often very different, making a comparison much harder than it needs to be.

issue_title: [QUESTION] What values should we use for our user agent?
issue_body:
This came up in #2367. In [node](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/blob/d24209a660706b70fee7d18ece9ca7a26d2f0403/packages/common/src/util.js#L624-L630), they use `gcloud-node-{SERVICE}/{SERVICE_VERSION}`. So for example, [bigtable](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/blob/d24209a660706b70fee7d18ece9ca7a26d2f0403/packages/bigtable/package.json#L2-L3) would be `gcloud-node-bigtable/0.3.1`.

/cc @callmehiphop @stephenplusplus

issue_title: Issue when inserting Stackdriver sink identity in Bigquery dataset
issue_body:
Hello,

I was going through the [documentation](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/logging-usage.html) for setting up a Stackdriver sink using the python API and had some issues so I decided to ask here if I did something wrong (I got the answer already in [SO](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43688393/bigquery-not-accepting-stackdrivers-sink-writer-identity?noredirect=1#comment74430872_43688393) but I'm not sure what happened yet).

The code I used:

```python
from google.cloud.logging.client import Client as lClient
from google.cloud.bigquery.client import Client as bqClient
from google.cloud.bigquery.dataset import AccessGrant

import os
os.environ['GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS'] = 'path_to_json.json'

lc = lClient()
bqc = bqClient()

ds = bqc.dataset('working_dataset')
acc = ds.access_grants
#this is the service account that is shown in our Exports tab in GCP.
acc.append(
    AccessGrant('WRITER', 'groupByEmail', 'cloud-logs@system.gserviceaccount.com'))
ds.access_grants = acc
ds.update()

```

I followed the docs but then I got the error when trying to update Bigquery's dataset:

```
NotFound: 404 Not found: Email cloud-logs@system.gserviceaccount.com (PUT 
https://www.googleapis.com/bigquery/v2/projects/[project-id]/datasets/[working-dataset])
```

As Xuejian helped me, this account is not a _group_ one but rather a _user_ one. I tried changing it but then got the error:

```
BadRequest: 400 Cannot remove all owners from a dataset.
```

So I decided to create a brand new access_grants for my dataset object and this time I put all accounts related to it, something like:

```python
acc = [
    AccessGrant('OWNER', 'userByEmail', 'owner who created dataset email'),
    AccessGrant('WRITER', 'userByEmail', 'cloud-logs@system.gserviceaccount.com'),
]
```

And this time it worked. So I was wondering, when we run `ds.access_grants` shouldn't it already bring all accesses related to this dataset? Also should the documentation be updated with **userByEmail** instead of **groupByEmail** when creating the access of the sink's writer?

My setup was Python 3.6. from latest jessie docker image

Thanks,

issue_title: Replace loops over the closure of multiple targets with a shared graph walk
issue_body:
Rather than `t.closure(bfs=True) for t in targets`, it is much more efficient to use something like `build_graph.transitive_subgraph_of_addresses_bfs(t.address for t in targets)`. We should add a helper to do this at a higher level, and audit the codebase post [r/3036](https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/3036/) (and post [r/3158](https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/3158/)) to use the high level helper.

Currently, to use it you need to do something like:

```
+    if root_targets:
+      build_graph = iter(root_targets).next()._build_graph
+      addresses = [t.address for t in root_targets]
+      closure = build_graph.transitive_subgraph_of_addresses_bfs(addresses)
+    else:
+      closure = set()
```

issue_title: Support for periodically GC'ing a local buildcache instance
issue_body:
`local_artifact_cache.py` should gain support for periodically clearing out "old" artifacts.

The primary usecase is for automated processes (like CI) which build things almost constantly, and which may have disk usage constraints. But this also benefits users who dislike unbounded growth of build support directories.

It's possible that the change should be made generically in `artifact_cache.py`, which would allow callers to set up a background task generically via `run_tracker.py` and `worker_pool.py`.

issue_title: Pip install from cache does not include KCL jars
issue_body:
We noticed that after the recent changes (fix of #28) the `amazon_kclpy` package has a bug regarding downloads of JAR files.

Steps to reproduce:

```
$ cd /tmp
$ virtualenv .venv
$ . .venv/bin/activate
$ pip install --no-cache amazon_kclpy
$ ls .venv/lib/python*/site-packages/amazon_kclpy/jars
__init__.py amazon-kinesis-client-1.7.2.jar aws-java-sdk-cloudwatch-1.11.14.jar ...
$ pip uninstall -y amazon_kclpy
$ pip install amazon_kclpy
$ ls .venv/lib/python*/site-packages/amazon_kclpy/jars
__init__.py
```

After the last step, the JAR files are missing in the `jars` directory. Would be great to get this fixed because most people will probably not use the `--no-cache` option on `pip install`, then the library becomes unusable.

issue_title: Docs are missing discovery API explanation
issue_body:
I found the answer in the code, but would have preferred to find it in the docs ;)

In short, for those looking for an answer: Discovery service should return to url in _return_ parameter and return param _entityID_ for the chosen entityID.

On a sidenote: in order to shortcut the discovery service in test environment I pointed the discovery service URL back to the disco_response endpoint (/Saml2/disco in example) containing a preselected entityID. However, the create_discovery_service_request itself adds an entityID param as well, so there is some contamination that could have been prevented by choosing another consumer parameter for return entityID. I solved it by returning and checking for _eID_. Also, pysaml2 has a bug for discovery service url's containing a querypart, which I already created here https://github.com/rohe/pysaml2/issues/402

issue_title: django 1.8 patch
issue_body:
diff --git a/django_pyodbc/base.py b/django_pyodbc/base.py
index f467417..02f8ee5 100644
--- a/django_pyodbc/base.py
+++ b/django_pyodbc/base.py
@@ -60,6 +60,17 @@ from django_pyodbc.introspection import DatabaseIntrospection
 DatabaseError = Database.Error
 IntegrityError = Database.IntegrityError
 
+class DataTypesWrapper(dict):
+    def __getitem__(self, item):
+        if item in ('PositiveIntegerField', 'PositiveSmallIntegerField'):
+            # The check name must be unique for the database. Add a random
+            # component so the regresion tests don't complain about duplicate names
+            fldtype = {'PositiveIntegerField': 'int', 'PositiveSmallIntegerField': 'smallint'}[item]
+            rnd_hash = md5_constructor(b(str(random.random()))).hexdigest()
+            unique = base64.b64encode(b(rnd_hash), b('__'))[:6]
+            return '%(fldtype)s CONSTRAINT [CK_%(fldtype)s_pos_%(unique)s_%%(column)s] CHECK ([%%(column)s] >= 0)' % locals()
+        return super(DataTypesWrapper, self).__getitem__(item)
+
 class DatabaseFeatures(BaseDatabaseFeatures):
     can_use_chunked_reads = False
     can_return_id_from_insert = True
@@ -94,6 +105,42 @@ class DatabaseWrapper(BaseDatabaseWrapper):
     Database = Database
     limit_table_list = False
 
+# Any format strings starting with "qn_" are quoted before being used in the
+    # output (the "qn_" prefix is stripped before the lookup is performed.
+
+    data_types = DataTypesWrapper({
+        'AutoField':                    'int IDENTITY (1, 1)',
+        'BigAutoField':                 'bigint IDENTITY (1, 1)',
+        'BigIntegerField':              'bigint',
+        'BinaryField':                  'varbinary(max)',
+        'BooleanField':                 'bit',

issue_title: Namespace issue with pyspark.ml and pyspark.mllib
issue_body:
I tried to run the default example on the README page
```
from sklearn import svm, grid_search, datasets
from spark_sklearn import GridSearchCV
iris = datasets.load_iris()
parameters = {'kernel':('linear', 'rbf'), 'C':[1, 10]}
svr = svm.SVC()
clf = GridSearchCV(sc, svr, parameters)
clf.fit(iris.data, iris.target)
```
on Spark, but got the following error:
```
ImportError: No module named linalg
```
The code that causes this is the import of `pyspark.ml.linalg` [on this line in converter.py in spark_sklearn](https://github.com/databricks/spark-sklearn/blob/master/python/spark_sklearn/converter.py#L16)

We are running Spark 1.6, and according to [the documentation](http://spark.apache.org/docs/1.6.0/api/python/index.html), in 1.6 and above, `linalg` is under `pyspark.mllib.linalg` instead of `pyspark.ml.linalg`.

I'm trying to figure out if it's an issue with my versions or what else exactly, given that the README mentions Spark 2.0 compatibility, but if this indeed an issue with spark_sklearn, it looks like this should be broken then since at least 1.6.0? Can someone confirm?

issue_title: Idea: Allow ustom sascfg.py location
issue_body:
Hi,

As far as I can tell it's not possible to override the location of where the config-file to look in when establishing the initial SAS-connection (if I'm mistaking, please let me know how - I've been unable to find it in the docs), which makes it harder to versionize and deploy saspy across different enviroments, since one usually wouldn't track changes in the module folder but only the code base. 

One could just put in all the parameters when constructing the connection, but it seems messy compared to maintaining a config-file. Alternatively one could store the config-file in the version-control system and override the package supplyed config on each pull but that again seems cumbersome compared to just pointing to the config-file in the code.

I'm not confident enough with my Python skills to do a pull-request myself, but I'd love to help out in any way I can.

issue_title: Evaluate possible path for making `endpoint server create` more uniform with `endpoint server delete`
issue_body:
@corpulentcoffee, I'm asking you to help look at this since you're reviewing #389 already.

Right now, `endpoint server create` takes a required option, `--hostname`.
e.g. `globus endpoint server create $epid --hostname foo.com`

We've gotten rid of basically all of our required options, but this one lingers.
I think we can change the command to better line up with `endpoint server delete $epid foo.com` by doing this:
- `--hostname` is an option which takes no values and does nothing
- usage becomes `globus endpoint server create ENDPOINT HOSTNAME`

Does this look right to you? Any current, correct usage of the command should continue to work fine, as we're replacing a required option with a positional arg, but stopping the option (now optional) from consuming a value.

`--hostname` would be marked deprecated and slated for removal in CLI v2

That's my reasoning, but I wanted to think about it/potentially discuss before even proposing the change in a PR.

issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The syncing code in `p2p.chain` under `BaseHeaderChainSyncer._sync` makes an assumption about the received headers which is not necessarily correct.

### What I think is going on (please confirm)

In trying to understand where these are coming from, I'm working to understand the syncing logic and want to be sure I have it correct.

- [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._sync#L205`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L205) requests headers from the chosen `peer`
- [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._fetch_missing_headers#L253`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L253) issues a request to the peer for some headers.
- [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._fetch_missing_headers#L256`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L256) waits for new headers to show up in the `_new_headers` queue.
- When the new headers show up, [`p2p.chain.BaseHeaderChainSyncer._handle_block_headers#L276`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L276) puts the new headers in the `_new_headers` queue.

So, IIUC, if **any** peer sends us a `BlockHeaders` message, the new headers will end up in the `_new_headers` queue and the `headers` returned in [`_fetch_missing_headers`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/chain.py#L256) are not guaranteed to originate from the peer they were requested from.

Alternate explanation of what I'm trying to understand.

1. We request headers from peer `A`
2. Peer `B` sends us a `BlockHeaders` message before peer `A` responds
3. The headers from peer `B` end up in the queue.
4. We treat the headers received from peer `B` as if they were sent from peer `A`.

If this is the case then I believe we have a systemic problem that we need to solve.

### How we can solve it (maybe)

My understanding is that there is no `id` for these types of requests so we cannot do `id` based linking between requests and responses.

Without an `id` based way to link requests and responses, I think we have to do it in a probabilistic manner.

1. instead of a single queue for headers, lets use a queue per peer.
2. use the [`PeerPoolSubscriber.deregister_peer`](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/3dbad6b8ea82b2f325b3fce6d96a7f0dc2b5642f/p2p/peer.py#L706) API to clear out stale queues.
3. maintain list of *header requests* that are awaiting responses.
4. on receipt of `BlockHeaders` message, we only put it in the queue if it has a corresponding *header request* which is satisfied by the headers contained in the message.

Defining *header request*:

- A *header request* would be defined as the requesting parameters in the `GetBlockHeaders` message.  
- A match would be defined as a non-empty set of headers which is a subset of the full set of headers that could be generated by the request.

Any received headers that cannot be correlated to a specific peer and header request should be logged and discarded.

issue_title: Too many files to create an environment
issue_body:
# Problems

## Too many files

Conda and anaconda have a growing number of files that can be used for different purposes, but achieve the basic functionality of creating or specifying an environment. 

Some formats add to this core, but they all share the same base.

| Command |  Instals packages  | Channel support | pip support | project support | platform specificity | different requirement syntax | 
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| `command line` | ✅  | ✅  | ✅  | ✅  | ⛔  | -  |
| [`requirements.txt`](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/using/envs.html#build-identical-conda-environments) | ✅  | ⛔  | ⛔  | ⛔  | ⛔  | -  |
| [`environment.yaml`](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/using/envs.html#use-environment-from-file) | ✅  | ✅  | ✅  | ⛔  | ⛔  | -  |
| [`project.yaml | kapsel.yaml`](https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-project/blob/conda-kapsel-staging/kapsel-config.rst) | ✅  | ✅  | ✅  | ✅  | ⛔  | -  |
| [`constructor.yaml`](https://github.com/conda/constructor/blob/master/CONSTRUCT.md) | ✅ | ✅  | ⛔  | ⛔  | ⛔  | -  |
| [`cas-installer.yaml`](https://docs.continuum.io/anaconda-repository/cli-cas-installer#cas-installer) | ✅ | ✅  | ⛔  | ⛔  | ⛔  | -  |
| [`meta.yaml`](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/building/meta-yaml.html) | ✅  | ✅  | ⛔  | ⛔  | ✅  | 💀   |

### Notes

 * [`meta.yaml`](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/building/meta-yaml.html)
   * ✅ can use requirements.txt if found

# Possible Solutions

### Lets standardize. 

As we move forward we should think about how adding new file formats and definitions affects the conda ecosystem. Are we going to have an ever growing list of file formats? Or should we deprecate a few in a planned and controlled way?

issue_title: ipython installation does not work properly in new environment
issue_body:
Hi,

I think there is a bug when ipython is installed within a new conda environment

I have python 3.4.3 in my root environment, now I want to create a new environment with python 2.7, so I execute

conda create -n test python=2.7
source activate test

which python    -> somepath/conda/envs/test/bin/python
which ipython   -> somepath/conda/bin/ipython


now, **I start ipython** and naturally it loads python 3.4.3 because its still using the ipython from the root installation, so I go

conda install ipython

which python    -> somepath/conda/envs/test/bin/python
which ipython   -> somepath/conda/envs/test/bin/ipython

when starting ipython now, it still loads the wrong python version (3.4.3. instead of 2.7). Note that this behaviour only occurs if the wrong ipython is started before the conda installation. So the problem can be avoided by installing ipython right from the start:

conda create -n test1 python=2.7 ipython

I don't understand why this happens but I though it might be worth mentioning it here.


Cheers!

issue_title: AttributeError: module 'pandas' has no attribute 'plotting' with tox
issue_body:
`pandas` 0.20.3 fails to import on every test when running tox.

- `tox` packages installed, besides my package:

`cycler==0.10.0, matplotlib==2.0.2, numexpr==2.6.2, numpy==1.13.1, pandas==0.20.3, py==1.4.34, pyparsing==2.2.0, pytest==3.2.2, pytest-travis-fold==1.2.0, python-dateutil==2.6.1, pytz==2017.2, scipy==0.19.1, six==1.10.0, tables==3.4.2`

- `platform linux -- Python 3.5.2, pytest-3.2.2, py-1.4.34, pluggy-0.4.0`

- all tests fail with the following error:

```
myfile.py:16: in <module>
    import pandas as pd
.tox/3.5-nocov/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pandas/__init__.py:51: in <module>
    plot_params = pandas.plotting._style._Options(deprecated=True)

Testing the same import in a `virtualenv` without `tox` works correctly.

Looks like it is a problem with `pandas`, but could also be something regarding `tox` (or even `pytest`). Hopefully, someone more experienced with `tox` can guide me here.

I have Python 3.5.3 in my conda root environment. Then I do virtualenv and try to execute some Python script with ``PYTHONWARNINGS=error``. Problem is that I get

```
$ PYTHONWARNINGS=error python foo.py
Failed to import the site module

    f = open(fullname, "rU")

$
```

The ``foo.py`` file is:

```
$ cat >foo.py <<EOF
> from random import randrange
> EOF

issue_title: Allow no value in configuration.
issue_body:
The `ConfigParser` module allows the following in its configuration files provided that the `allow_no_option` is set to `True`:

```
[Section]
value0
value1
value2
```

is there a way to do that with ConfigObj?

I know I could have something like

```
[Section]
values = value0, value1, value2
```

But that would mean changing all the configuration files we currently use which is not ideal as it would break any other code that uses the old `ConfigParser` set up.  The reason I wan to use `ConfigObj` is to validate the configuration files when they are read, not used.
